name: Morro de l'Abella and Salt de Molí-Bernat Circuit
name_original: Morro de l'Abella y cascada de Salt de Molí-Bernat - Circular en Tavertet
description: Hike the moderate 6.1-mile Morro de l'Abella and Salt de Molí-Bernat waterfall circular trail in Tavertet, offering panoramic views and a po
region_intro: Hiking around Tavertet, located in Catalonia's Osona region, offers access to dramatic landscapes and diverse natural features. The area is characterized by towering cliffs, some over 300 meters high, providing panoramic views over the Sau reservoir and the Guilleries massif. Terrain includes lush forests of oak and beech, alongside traditional farmhouses and olive groves. The region's unique topography features large precipices and rocky formations. Best hiking trails around Tavertet The most popular hiking route is Morro de l'Abella and Salt de Molí-Bernat Circuit, a 6.1 miles (9.9 km) trail that takes 3 hours 27 minutes to complete. This moderate route features views of the cliffs and a waterfall. Another top favourite among local hikers is From Tavertet to Sant Corneli - circular via Puig De La Cruz, a moderate 7.6 miles (12.2 km) path. It offers varied scenery through the local countryside and takes approximately 3 hours 44 minutes. Local hikers also love the Tavertet Viewpoint – View of the Cliffs of Tavertet loop from Tavertet, a 5.2 miles (8.4 km) trail leading through cliff-edge viewpoints, often completed in about 2 hours 56 minutes. Hiking around Tavertet is defined by dramatic cliffs, panoramic viewpoints, and forested paths. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy walks to more challenging routes. The routes in Tavertet are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 380 reviews. More than 2600 hikers have used komoot to explore Tavertet's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are there in Tavertet?
A: Tavertet offers a diverse network of over 30 hiking trails. These routes cater to various skill levels, from easy walks to more challenging excursions, allowing you to explore the dramatic landscapes of the Osona region.

Q: What are the difficulty levels of hikes in Tavertet?
A: The hiking trails around Tavertet range from easy to difficult. There are 9 easy routes, 18 moderate routes, and 3 difficult routes. This variety ensures options for all fitness levels, from casual walkers to experienced hikers seeking a challenge.

Q: Are there any impressive waterfalls to see on the trails?
A: Yes, Tavertet is home to several stunning waterfalls. You can discover cascades like the Salt del Sallent, which plunges over 100 meters, and the spectacular Salt del Molí Bernat, an overhanging waterfall of about 70 meters. The Tirabous cascade is also a notable sight. Many routes, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e924875887/morro-de-l-abella-and-salt-de-moli-bernat-circuit" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Morro de l'Abella and Salt de Molí-Bernat Circuit&lt;/a&gt;, incorporate these natural wonders.

Q: What kind of views can I expect while hiking in Tavertet?
A: Hikers in Tavertet are treated to breathtaking panoramic views. The village is perched on towering cliffs, offering unparalleled vistas over the Sau reservoir, the Guilleries massif, and the Collsacabra mountain range. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10470825" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tavertet Viewpoint – View of the Cliffs of Tavertet loop&lt;/a&gt; specifically highlight these dramatic clifftop perspectives.

Q: Are there any historical or unique landmarks to explore near the trails?
A: Absolutely. Beyond the natural beauty, you can explore unique landmarks. The partially submerged &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2679945" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Sant Romà de Sau&lt;/a&gt;, visible depending on water levels in the Sau reservoir, offers a haunting sight. The charming medieval village of Tavertet itself, with its preserved stone houses, is a historical gem. Additionally, the region features unique geological formations like the "Pedra de les Bruixes" (Stone of the Witches) with medieval engravings.

Q: Can I find circular hiking routes in Tavertet?
A: Yes, many of the trails in Tavertet are circular, offering convenient starting and ending points in the village. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e980211599/from-tavertet-to-sant-corneli-circular-via-puig-de-la-cruz" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;From Tavertet to Sant Corneli - circular via Puig De La Cruz&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6956768" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Cliffs of Tavertet – Salt del Molí Bernat loop&lt;/a&gt;, allowing you to experience diverse scenery without retracing your steps.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Tavertet?
A: The hiking routes in Tavertet are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 380 reviews. More than 2600 hikers have used komoot to explore Tavertet's varied terrain, often praising the dramatic cliffs, panoramic viewpoints, and the well-maintained forested paths.

Q: Is Tavertet suitable for family hikes?
A: Yes, Tavertet offers several easy and accessible paths suitable for families. While some routes involve dramatic cliffs, there are options that provide a gentler experience through lush forests and traditional landscapes, perfect for a family outing. Always check the specific trail details for suitability.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Tavertet?
A: Tavertet is beautiful year-round, but spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant hiking conditions with mild temperatures and vibrant natural colors. Summer can be warm, so early morning or late afternoon hikes are recommended. Winter hiking is also possible, offering crisp air and unique views, though some paths might be slippery.

Q: Where can I park when hiking in Tavertet?
A: Tavertet village typically has designated parking areas for visitors. It's advisable to arrive early, especially on weekends or during peak season, as parking can fill up. Many popular trailheads are accessible directly from the village.

Q: Is it possible to reach Tavertet by public transport for hiking?
A: While Tavertet is somewhat remote, it is generally accessible by public transport, though options might be limited. It's best to check local bus schedules from larger nearby towns like Vic, which often have connections to smaller villages in the Osona region. Planning ahead is recommended.

Q: Are there any longer hikes for experienced hikers?
A: Yes, for experienced hikers, Tavertet offers more challenging routes. Trails like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/17552822" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tavertet Viewpoint – Church of Sant Romà de Sau loop&lt;/a&gt;, at nearly 12 km with significant elevation gain, provide a good test of endurance and offer extended exploration of the dramatic clifftop scenery and the Sau reservoir area.

Q: What other natural attractions are near the hiking trails?
A: Beyond the immediate trails, the region around Tavertet boasts several other natural attractions. You can explore the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/guide/3819950/lakes-around-tavertet" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lakes around Tavertet&lt;/a&gt;, including the Sau Reservoir itself, and discover various &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/guide/3819948/gorges-around-tavertet" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gorges around Tavertet&lt;/a&gt;, featuring additional waterfalls and unique geological formations like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/380370" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Foradada Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;.
tips:
The church of Sant Cristòfol de Tavertet (San Cristóbal de Tavertet) is located in the term of Tavertet in the Catalan region of Osona, in Spain.

   It appears documented, in the year 1070, as the possession of the Viscount of Cardona and was consecrated to Saint Christopher.

   Originally from the 11th century, it consisted of a single nave with a barrel vault, followed and reinforced by three transverse arches that divided it into four sections and a semicircular apse with a quarter-sphere vault.

   During the 12th and 13th centuries, the nave was lengthened and the bell tower was added, with a cross-shaped window. In the lateral Romanesque wall, through three semicircular arches, the lateral nave with Gothic arches was opened. In the 18th century, the southern sacristy was added. Possibly, the raised envelope of the apse also corresponds to this moment. At the end of the 20th century, the Romanesque apparatus that had been covered was uncovered and the apse was returned to its original state.
Beautiful viewpoint from where we can contemplate the grandeur of the "Balà" stream, the "Molí-Bernat" waterfall and the "Les Corts" bauma.
The passage of the waterfall is internal but there is a chain to hold onto in case of moisture.
Ladder fixed to the rock of approx. 5 meters in total, which will allow us to climb to the top of the balma. The ladder is solid, although it is not recommended for people with a lot of vertigo.

Caves that during the Neolithic and until the Iberian period had been a lookout and hunting settlement (complementary to the town of Pla del Castell).
Considered one of the oldest documented stable occupations, dating from the year 3100 to 900 before our era.
Although no human remains have been found, the discovery in this lower level of a prehistoric home and a fauna deposit (with the supposed remains of a wild boar half consumed by the ancient settlers) will help to study what life was like in this space six years ago. a thousand years.
Amazing views over the Pantà de Sau. Beware if you have vertigo!

faqs.question: How difficult is the Morro de l'Abella and Salt de Molí-Bernat waterfall circular hike?
faqs.answer: This trail is generally rated as moderate in difficulty. It requires a good level of fitness and sure-footedness due to varied terrain, including rocky paths and some steep sections. Hikers should be prepared for potential slippery surfaces, especially after rain. Some parts may involve equipped passes in aerial sections, which might be challenging for those with vertigo.

faqs.question: What kind of views and natural features can I expect to see along this route?
faqs.answer: You'll experience spectacular scenery, including breathtaking panoramic views of the Sau reservoir from Morro de l'Abella, the dramatic cliffs of Tavertet, and surrounding mountains. A major highlight is the impressive Salt del Molí Bernat waterfall, which cascades about 70 meters. You'll also pass by the Church of Sant Cristòfol de Tavertet, the Cave of the Pixarelles, and the View of the Riera de Balà, Molí-Bernat Waterfall, and Bauma de Les Corts.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Morro de l'Abella and Salt de Molí-Bernat waterfall trail?
faqs.answer: The trail typically starts from the charming village of Tavertet. There is usually designated parking available within or near the village, which serves as a convenient starting point for this circular route. It's advisable to arrive early, especially on weekends or during peak season, to secure a spot.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this trail?
faqs.answer: The trail is enjoyable throughout much of the year. Spring offers lush greenery and full waterfalls, while autumn provides beautiful fall colors. Summer can be warm, so an early start is recommended. Winter hiking is possible, but be prepared for potentially colder temperatures and slippery conditions, especially near the waterfalls or on shaded rocky sections.

faqs.question: Is the Morro de l'Abella and Salt de Molí-Bernat waterfall circular trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, this trail is generally considered dog-friendly. However, given the moderate difficulty, varied terrain, and some equipped passes, ensure your dog is fit enough for the hike. Always keep your dog on a leash, especially near cliffs, wildlife, or other hikers, and be mindful of local regulations regarding pets in natural areas.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or fees required to hike in the Collsacabra area around Tavertet?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to hike this particular trail in the Collsacabra region. However, it's always good practice to check for any updated local regulations or conservation efforts, especially if visiting during peak times. Please respect the natural environment and follow all posted signs.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this circular route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is quite varied, offering a mix of accessible sections, unpaved trails, and some more challenging alpine-like areas. You'll encounter rocky paths, areas where you might need to navigate through water, and a steep, often shaded descent towards the Balà stream, followed by a climb back up to the Morro de l'Abella viewpoint. Good hiking boots with ankle support are highly recommended.

faqs.question: Does this trail intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route also passes through sections of the "Tavertet - Pla del Castell - Tavertet", "Camí Oliba", and "La Jonquera - Sant Adrià del Besós" trails. This offers opportunities to explore further or combine parts of different routes if you wish to extend your hike.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities like cafes or restrooms near the trailhead in Tavertet?
faqs.answer: The village of Tavertet, where the trail often begins, offers some amenities. You can typically find cafes and restaurants in the village where you can grab a bite or a drink before or after your hike. Restroom facilities are usually available in these establishments or public areas within the village.

faqs.question: Is it possible to pass directly under the Salt del Molí Bernat waterfall?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is possible to pass directly beneath the Salt del Molí Bernat waterfall. Be aware that doing so will likely involve getting wet, especially after heavy rains when the waterfall is at its most powerful. It's an exhilarating experience but proceed with caution.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this hike?
faqs.answer: Given the moderate difficulty and varied terrain, we recommend bringing sturdy hiking boots, sufficient water, snacks, and appropriate clothing layers for changing weather conditions. A waterproof jacket is advisable, especially if you plan to pass under the waterfall. A map or GPS device (like the komoot app) is also useful for navigation.
